title: What Comes Next: Computer Vision
concept: Computer vision applies the same deep-learning foundations to images: pixels become tensors, layers learn visual features, optimization trains on labeled examples, and image-focused architectures turn the general toolkit into systems that can classify, detect, and segment the visual world.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why is image augmentation often useful in deep learning?

options.text: It helps the model generalize by showing useful variation instead of one rigid version of every example.
It removes the need for labels and evaluation.
It guarantees the model will be small enough for any phone.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Augmentation is one of the tools that helps a model handle more of the variation it will meet in real images.

cards.title: Now the network learns to see
cards.body: Everything you learned about layers, activations, loss, optimization, regularization, transfer learning, and scaling still matters in computer vision. The main difference is the kind of data: instead of small tables or abstract vectors, the network now learns from grids of pixels that contain edges, textures, shapes, and scenes.

cards.title: What computer vision adds
cards.body: Computer vision is the branch of AI where models learn from images or video. The deep-learning foundations stay the same, but now the model must extract visual structure from pixel data. That leads to image-friendly designs such as convolutions, residual blocks, transfer from pretrained vision models, augmentation pipelines, and deployment choices for cameras, phones, or edge devices.

cards.title: From letters to whole pictures
cards.body: If earlier lessons were like learning how language is built from letters, words, and sentences, computer vision is like learning how a picture is built from corners, edges, textures, objects, and scenes. The logic is still pattern learning — the patterns are just visual now.

cards.title: Different visual tasks, same core engine
cards.body: A vision model might classify an image as cat or dog, detect where a pedestrian is in a street photo, or segment every pixel that belongs to a road. Those tasks look different, but underneath they still depend on good representations, sensible losses, stable training, regularization, and strong evaluation.

cards.title: Computer vision is not a totally separate universe
cards.body: A common myth is that vision starts from scratch with completely different rules. It does not. Vision adds image-specific architectures and tasks, but it still rests on the same deep-learning foundations you already built. That is why this capstone matters: you are not starting over, you are applying your toolkit to a new kind of data.

cards.title: What stays the same vs. what becomes image-specific
cards.body: Computer vision is a handoff, not a reset.

left.text:
Layers, activations, loss
Backprop, optimizers, schedules
Regularization and transfer

right.text:
Pixels and image tensors
Classification, detection, segmentation
Image architectures and deployment

exercises.prompt: When you move into computer vision, which part of your toolkit still matters immediately?

options.text: Losses, optimizers, regularization, and the idea of learning useful representations.
Only the camera hardware, because training concepts no longer matter.
None of the earlier deep-learning lessons, because images are not data.
Just the learning-rate schedule, since architectures and losses disappear in vision.

exercises.explain: Correct. Vision adds image-specific ideas, but the old training toolkit absolutely still matters.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Computer vision throws away backpropagation and optimization because pictures are too visual for those ideas.
exercises.explain: False. Vision models still learn through loss, gradients, and weight updates just like other deep networks.

exercises.sentence: In computer vision, grids of pixels are turned into image ___.
exercises.explain: Tensors is correct. Images become structured numeric arrays the network can process.

exercises.prompt: Which project sounds most like a computer-vision task built on your deep-learning foundations?

options.text: A street-camera model learns from labeled images to detect pedestrians and must balance representation quality, loss choice, and deployment speed.
A team says image models need no evaluation because humans can already see the photos.
A project decides pixels are too messy, so it refuses to learn from visual data at all.

exercises.explain: Exactly. That is computer vision in practice: image data plus the same model-building judgment you already know.

exercises.prompt: If you understand perceptrons, hidden layers, activations, loss, backpropagation, optimization, regularization, transfer learning, and deployment tradeoffs, what is the most natural next step?

options.text: Apply those same foundations to images and video by learning computer-vision architectures and tasks.
Abandon deep learning entirely, because none of those ideas transfer to visual problems.
Focus only on camera brands, since architecture and optimization no longer matter.
Assume every visual task is already solved as long as you know what a pixel is.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Computer vision is the next natural handoff: same deep-learning engine, new visual data and new task types. One lesson remains — your Day 450 graduation, where this whole foundations arc closes and the vision phase begins.
